era_context: The early 12th century: the Crusader States were consolidating in the Levant following the First Crusade (1096–1099); the Song Dynasty was in its cultural and technological golden age; the Seljuk Empire dominated the Near East; the Jurchen were consolidating power in Manchuria (Jin Dynasty would be founded in 1115); Islamic scholarship flourished across the Islamic world.

events.title: Crusader military campaigns in the Levant under Baldwin II
events.region: Kingdom of Jerusalem and surrounding Crusader territories
events.description: King Baldwin II of Jerusalem (r. 1100–1118) continued military campaigns to expand and consolidate Crusader territorial control. In 1112, Baldwin was engaged in campaigns against Muslim forces and in establishing Crusader fortifications. The Crusaders were consolidating their hold on the Levant following the First Crusade.
key_figures: Joscelin I of Courtenay (Count of Edessa)
events.certainty_note: Crusader military activity in this period is documented, though specific campaigns in 1112 are not always precisely dated.

events.title: Song Dynasty cultural and technological flourishing
events.description: The Northern Song Dynasty continued its period of extraordinary cultural, technological, and artistic achievement. In 1112, advances in ceramics, printing, mathematics, and astronomy were ongoing. Emperor Huizong (r. 1100–1126) was a major patron of the arts and an accomplished painter and calligrapher.

sources.name: Song Shi (History of the Song Dynasty)

events.certainty_note: Song Dynasty cultural achievements are well documented.

events.title: Seljuk Empire under Muhammad I Tapar
events.description: Muhammad I Tapar (r. 1105–1118) ruled the Great Seljuk Empire during a period of consolidation and cultural flourishing. The Seljuk state was at the height of its territorial extent and political power.
events.certainty_note: Seljuk history in this period is well documented in Persian and Islamic sources.

events.title: Jurchen consolidation in Manchuria
events.description: The Jurchen people under Aguda were consolidating their political and military organization in the years leading up to the founding of the Jin Dynasty in 1115. By 1112, Aguda had established himself as the paramount leader of the Jurchen tribes and was preparing for the formal proclamation of the Jin Dynasty.

sources.name: Jin Shi (History of the Jin Dynasty)

events.certainty_note: The consolidation of Jurchen power in the years before 1115 is documented, though specific events in 1112 are not always precisely dated.

events.title: Islamic scholarship and cultural flourishing
events.description: In 1112, Islamic scholarship was flourishing across the Islamic world. Scholars in Al-Andalus, Persia, Egypt, and Syria were producing significant works in mathematics, astronomy, medicine, philosophy, and theology.

sources.name: Islamic historical chronicles and biographical dictionaries

events.certainty_note: The flourishing of Islamic scholarship in this period is well documented.

disconfirming_evidence: No significant disconfirming evidence identified for the events listed.
historiographic_note: Documentation for 1112 CE is moderate. Crusader history is well documented through contemporary chronicles. Song Dynasty history is richly documented. Seljuk and Islamic history are moderately documented. Jurchen history in the pre-Jin period is less well documented than post-1115 history. Ethiopian, sub-Saharan African, and pre-Columbian American history for this year are essentially undocumented in surviving written sources.

graph_edges.note: Jurchen consolidation in 1112 led to formal founding of Jin Dynasty in 1115
